feed.title: kairos@net:~
feed.subtitle: A jekyll theme inspired by linux consoles for hackers, developers and script kiddies.

entry.title: Spiderland: a post-rock masterpiece
entry.content:
Spiderland: a post-rock masterpiece

Jonathan Kocevar April 21, 2022 Reading Time: approx. 3 min.

    Recently I have been in quite a writers block. I have many writing projects on the go but have really slowed down recently doing alot of research. I have been looking for new passionate topics to post about for the shorter, more heartfelt posts on the page. I figure I should write about music, and thought why not write about one of my favourite albums that I have been listening to for a few years but only recently came to really truly appreciate? That being Louisville band Slint’s sophomore album, Spiderland1. The relatively short album, only spanning 6 songs over 39 minutes, is an extremely heavy experience. I’ve listened to the album over 100 times through, and love it just as if it was my first time. I’ll give a track-by-track rundown of what I thought on each piece below.

    The first track of the album, Breadcrumb Trail introduces the truly revolutionary sound of the album. The dynamic high but soft sound really shines on the track when combined with the spoken word poem of a boy and a fortune teller riding a roller coaster and sharing the moment. The screeching of the guitars mimicking the highs and lows of the coaster as the boy and fortune teller ride sound like nothing I’ve heard much before when in combination with the soft, calm lows immediately following.

    The second track, Nosferatu Man, labelled after the cult-classic horror Nosferatu (1922) expands on the low but rough instrumentals. Imagery of vampires and creatures haunt the lyrics of the spoken word from vocalist Brian McMahan, while the guitars almost sound like they rip in and out of the track, outputting well timed riffs offset from each other. The drums on the track sound like they take a lot more of an active role as well, playing harder and louder into the chorus.

    Don, Aman the third track, focuses on the story of Don, a man disillusioned from his surroundings at a party. The lyrics paint Don as isolated and seperate from the group, reflecting rather then enjoying the moment with the rest of his peers. He leaves the party, driving home finding solace and companionship in himself, “In the mirror, he saw his friend”. The instrumentals are relatively tame albiet the uneasy time signature, compared to the rest of the album, but the real focus is on the lyrics. McMahan seemingly reflects much of himself lyrically on the track, sharing thoughts of isolation, fear of social anxiety, and the social standards put upon him. I wholeheartedly enjoy this track every time, and I feel that almost anyone can slightly relate to the social anxiety of Don.

    The fourth track on the album, Washer is a calm yet lyrically heavy piece for the album. The build up on the track is slow and eerie, while the lyrics allude to thoughts of suicide and the narrator embracing death. I absolutely loved this track. The instrumentals creeping steadily towards the final dramatic burst of symphonic guitar clashes bring so much life (ironically) to the track. The guitar lead on the last half of the track burst in and scream, diluting quickly after out into the end of the track. While it is the lengthiest song on the short album, I find it to be one of my favourites as well.

    For Dinner…, the fifth track is a quiet instrumental which almost feels like a settling of the intensity of the end of Washer. Its calm and in pace, but keeps the ominous and depressing feel of the album. It also serves great as the mellow track before the deep finale on the album.

    The album ends with Good Morning, Captain an absolutely lyrically brilliant and intricate track to tie together the whole record. The guitars begin as what I can only describe as tinny and sinewy, while the thick bassline plays against it. McMahan’s quiet and calm spoken word contrasts the thundering instrumentals. The lyrics paint the story of a captain in total disarray, recovering from the destruction of his ship with seemingly no survivors. The story shifts near the end of the track, with the narrator seemingly in distress over regret and a desire to rekindle some sort of relationship, trying to “find my way home” and pleading for forgiveness. The familiar heaviness and rawness of the guitar and bass like on the tracks Breadcrumb Trail and Washer kicks back in near the ending of the track, The narrator viscerally screaming “I miss you” over top sounds metaphorically like they are almost drowning in the sound. It truly sounds wicked and tortured. The poetic evilness of this track sums up the album incredibly well.

    I also really recommend listening to the remastered editions additional tracks. The cover of Neil Young’s Cortez the Killer live from Chicago is really heartfelt and deep cut. I will admit I don’t think that McMahan’s vocal style is the best for the song but the instrumentals are amazing. The additional tracks also cover a bunch of demos (Ive heard that they were all recorded on a tape recorder in the back of an SUV, which if so only makes the songs more impressive) and has a few demos of songs from their first album Tweez, my favourite being Glenn.

    I hope this post has convinced you to take a look into Spiderland and Slint’s other works and be introduced to one of the biggest influences on modern rock and post-rock. The dark and evil themes overlaying the whole album are wonderful and the sound is revolutionary. I recently restriked my love for this album and see myself continuing to enjoy it way into the future. Below I have linked some interesting videos I found in relation to the album, check them out :)




  1. Spiderland on Bandcamp, Spiderland on Spotify ↩
entry.summary: Thoughts on both philosophy of the post-bloc West and the current Ukraine crisis

entry.title: Ukraine: a product of the West’s arrogance

link.title: Ukraine: a product of the West’s arrogance

entry.content:
Ukraine: a product of the West’s arrogance

Jonathan Kocevar April 21, 2022 Reading Time: approx. 3 min.

    With the war in Ukraine, conflict has come uncomfortably close to the nations of the West, sitting next to the dividing line between Eastern and Central Europe. How was this possible? According to the West, the living moments of the past 30 years have been championed as the “end of history”, and the end of war as the decisive factor in world conflicts. How has war once again emerged from its dormant status allowing for another strongman in Europe to have his way? The answer is it has never left.

    I believe there is an extremely important lesson of the Western psyche when analyzing the Ukrainian conflict, and it both shows a contradiction in the values and flaws of the West. Since the fall of the Berlin wall in 1989, the geopolitical state of the world was seen as post-historical. Liberal cries of “diplomacy triumphs over war” were seen as the new status of the globalized world. It very much mirrored the state of Western politics after the First World War, the uniting of nations in international bodies and a general agreeance to settle disputes with councils rather than cannons.

    With the encroachment of Russia into the territories of Ukraine within the past 20 years, Liberal democracy, the child of Western enlightened values, has failed again. The aggressive enforcement and encouragement of democratic states within the former Soviet Union and the policy of NATO enlargement by the Clinton regime in the mid-‘90s led to high tension within the area, with not much improvement to show. In turn, Ukraine has become another sacrificial goat for the West. It is another victim of NATO encroachment on the region, stuck in the unfortunate position of being wedged in between the major diplomatic factions of the world. Philosopher and cultural critic Slavoj Žižek, when prosed with the political nature of the Ukrainian conflict said the following:

“Look at all this Putin, Western Europe, United States madness! Are we aware that we are already entering the climate of the next world war? Our situation is not as people claim, that now we have new fascism, it’s like Western Europe in the nineteen-thirties. No! I think a much better metaphor is the years before the First Great War, where everyone was talking about the war, and then the big surprise is that the war really exploded! Why? because everyone is talking about it, but no one took it seriously.”

Žižek makes a well-founded assertion of the West here. It has not learned from its mistakes. The western notion of “Debate and argument of war will withhold it”, and that peace can be found solely through reason is false. War strikes those who are complacent with the notion that war is an event that happens to the other, people not close to the West or its values. The advertisement of Ukraine on the news and the placement of Ukraine as a sacrificial goat that Russia encroaches on exposes the hypocrisy of the West’s values once more. It differs much from the “military exercises” partaken in foreign countries like Syria and Yemen because that has “just been the way of life for those people”.

    To conclude, I hope for this article not to be seen as a dismissal of Ukrainian suffering and invasion, but rather as a small analysis of the hypocrisy of the West in terms of the Donbas conflict.
entry.summary: Thoughts on both philosophy of the post-bloc West and the current Ukraine crisis

entry.title: The stdin problem
entry.content:
The stdin problem

Jonathan Kocevar April 17, 2022 Reading Time: approx. 2 min.

    As the time of writing this, I am a first year student working on elementary C code, nothing too extravagant, but hopefully this little writeup can help someone or serve as a reference for people that were looking for an answer.

    Anyway, stdin, the main stream directed usually by the keyboard, is the stream taken from the console from DOS/Windows and *nix systems. While extremely useful for user input and interactivity, in the C language I have had multiple problems using it efficiently combined with the standard library.

    My main problem would come from reading an input from functions like scanf. While fine to use for smaller undefined strings, if scanf reads over a select amount it will hold the rest of the character inputs in stdin for the next scan, which can interfere with the programmers next calls to scanf or reference to stdin. I have created a simple function to “flush” the contents of stdin after reading it using scanf or another IO function for clean reference on the next call:


Now this solution may not be the most elegant or quickest, but when developing it I made it easy to read, and cover my cases. It simply initializes a char variable, called currentChar, and uses it as an ittorator to clean the overflowed characters from the function that read stdin before (whether it be scanf, or fgets). It grabs the current value from stdin with fgetc(stdin); and immediately checks if it is equal to a newline (which would be the user hitting “enter”), or EOF, a standard library macro which indicates an END OF FILE for the stream. If it passes either of these 2 cases, it breaks from the loop and ends the function. This function successfully flushes the stdin stream and allows for new clean inputs.
entry.summary: My struggles with the stdin buffer, and a few scuffed fixes for them

entry.title: Politics and Religion in Ancient Rome

link.title: Politics and Religion in Ancient Rome

entry.content:
Politics and Religion in Ancient Rome

Jonathan Kocevar April 3, 2022 Reading Time: approx. 4 min.

    Apollo, Jupiter, Mars, Juno. Infamous and well known gods of the even more infamous state of Rome, one of the largest geopolitical entities of its time. The religion and spiritual tales of Rome were a large inspiration and influence on the leaders and administrators of the state through its existence. Of the three periods of Roman political history, those being the Regal, Republican, and Imperial periods, religion and spiritual beliefs in Rome played a large part in the ever shifting nature of Roman politics and governance throughout its history.

    In the early years of the Roman state, the years of the reign of the mythical Romulus to Lucius Tarquinius Superbus, religion embeds itself as a deep part of Roman life and culture. While little records of the early kingdom’s history survive, oral lore and tradition documented by Roman and Greek historians such as Livy and Dionysius of Halicarnassus help to explain the importance of religion within the newly growing state. The introduction of many of the traditions of spirituality begin with its second King, Numa Pompilius. Departing from the founder and first King Romulus’s philosophies, Numa Popilius focused inward on Roman society and developing Roman religion. Numa began by redelegating many of the roles of the then unorganized religion of Rome, starting with appointing flamen, or priests, to the gods Jupiter, Mars, and Quirinus.1 In Livy’s great compilation of Roman history Ab urbe condita, he documents Numa Popilius’s creation of the priestesses of Vesta, also known as the Vestal Virgins, the infamous keepers of the sacred fire of Vesta and figureheads of the security of Rome.2 Numa Popilius is also known for the construction of the temple to the god Janus, which was used as a sign to the general public as an indicator of war within Rome, and with Numas reign “it was not seen open for a single day, but remained shut for the space of forty-three years together, so complete and universal was the cessation of war.”3 This shows a large sign of the early politics of Rome being deeply integrated with the presence of the gods and their will. Historian Plutarch writes of another office created by Numa, the Fetailes, which were a quasi-political priesthood, serving as diplomats calling on the gods’ approval when negotiations had gone hostile with surrounding tribes, even being the ones to produce the proclamations of war.4 With that, foreign relations of regal and early republican Rome would be under the hand of the Fetailes and the gods. It is clear that religion played a large part in early Roman kingship and political history, and was heavily intertwined with King Numa and his successors.

    The emergence of new religious practices, especially from tribes and peoples conquered and politically subservient to Rome, started to blossom during the Republican Era. Greek gods such as Hera, Artemis, and Ares, were adapted by the Romans to the gods Juno, Diana, and Mars, the gods of state, hunt, and war respectively.5 The adaptation and expansion of worship of politically tied Greek gods to the early Roman republic coincides with the large conquests of early Rome, such as Magna Gracia, Macedonia, and Hispania. After the heavy political and military defeat at the battle of Cannae during the second Punic war, the Roman public saw itself not in favor with the gods. Upon the consulship of Gaius Claudius Nero and Marcus Livius in 207 BC, a series of religious appeasements were to be made, hoping to switch political favor of the gods back to the Romans.6 The sacrifice of 4 adult men to the gods was deemed as the solution by the pontiffs.7 With the favor of the gods, Roman political stability had been reestablished, and the following years after 207 BC would see Carthage pushed out of Italia and Hispania by the Romans until the end of the war in 201 BC.8 In the late republic, many notable families of Rome, such as the Antonius, Pompeia, and Julia traced their lineage back to certain gods as a political and prestigious tactic. For example, the son of Roman General Pompey the Great, Sextus Pompey, had “assumed a certain additional glory and pride by representing himself to be son of Neptune, since his father had once ruled the whole sea.”9 The notion of aligning oneself with the gods was a symbolic alignment of the family with the god’s powers and historic precedent for the family within the republic’s political apparatus.10

    The Imperial Era of Roman history only continues the conjoining of state and politics with spirituality and religion. Many of the religious traditions started to become subservient, being either adapted or left behind to serve the Emperor’s political cause. Over the great transformative period that was Emperor Augustus’s reign, he looked to absorb sectors of the Roman religion into his rule. Directly after his settlement as Imperator, Augustus usurped the priestly title of Pontifex Maximus, a powerful Roman religious role and “decided to combine these two spheres symbolically by making part of his house on the Palatine Hill the official residence of the Pontifex Maximus.”11 This would make him a figurehead of political stability, as he had control over the election and appointing to the Vestal Virgins, who had since the origins of Rome been seen as icons of security and continuance of the Roman state.12 Beginning with Augustus and later strengthened by Emperors such as Diocletian, the formation of an Imperial cult, a mixing of the Emperors and the gods of Rome began.13 Much of the priests and Flamen from the Imperial Era, especially the Augustinian Era, were of aristocratic background, already serving in local imperial services and governmental positions. The political and religious structures merged through administration, as municipal nobles, doubling as flamen and priests, tended to both the imperial cult, and traditional Roman religious practices.

    In conclusion, religion was a decisive factor when regarding the governance of man and politics during all eras of Rome’s history. Through analysis of the Regal, Republican, and Imperial Eras of Roman political history, it is evident that the religious sectors of Rome played a large part in both the guidance and structure of Rome politically.

Works Cited


  1. Livy, Ab urbe condita 1.20.2 ↩

  2. “In like manner he designated virgins for Vesta’s service —a priesthood, this, that derived from Alba and so was not unsuited to the founder’s stock. That they might be perpetual priestesses of the temple, he assigned them a stipend from the public treasury, and by the rule of virginity and other observances invested them with awe and sanctity.”(Ibid. 1.20.3) ↩

  3. Plut. Num. 20.4.2 ↩

  4. “Roman Fetiales often went to those who were doing them a wrong and made personal appeals for fair treatment; but if the unfair treatment continued, they called the gods to witness, invoked many dreadful evils upon themselves and their country in case they resorted to hostilities unjustly, and so declared war upon them.”(Ibid. 12.4.1) ↩

  5. John Scheid and Janet Lloyd, in An Introduction to Roman Religion (Bloomington, IN: Indiana University Press, 2014), p. 179. ↩

  6. (Livy, Ab urbe condita 27.37.1) ↩

  7. “These prodigies were atoned for with full-grown victims, and a single day of prayer was observed by decree of the pontiffs. Then again the nine days of rites were repeated, because in the Armilustrium men saw a rain of stones.” (Ibid. 27.37.4) ↩

  8. Adrian Goldsworthy, in The Fall of Carthage: The Punic Wars 265-146 BC (London: Phoenix, 2009), p. 245. ↩

  9. L. de Blois and Peter Funke, in The Impact of Imperial Rome on Religions, Ritual, and Religious Life in the Roman Empire: Proceedings of the Fifth International Network, vol. 5 (Leiden: Brill, 2006), p. 24. ↩

  10. Edwin White Webster, in Virtus and Libertas: The Ideals and Spirit of the Roman Senatorial Aristocracy from the Punic Wars through the Time of Augustus (Chicago, Ill, 1936), pp. 29-30. ↩

  11. Werner Eck, in The Age of Augustus (Malden, Massachusetts: Blackwell Pub., 2007), pp. 74-75. ↩

  12. Ibid. ↩

  13. L. de Blois and Peter Funke, in The Impact of Imperial Rome on Religions, Ritual, and Religious Life in the Roman Empire: Proceedings of the Fifth International Network, vol. 5 (Leiden: Brill, 2006), pp. 41-42. ↩
entry.summary: An essay on the interrelation of spirituality and politics in Rome.

entry.title: Motfis of the Confessional Scene in The Conformist

link.title: Motfis of the Confessional Scene in The Conformist

entry.content:
Importance and Motfis of the Confessional Scene in The Conformist

Jonathan Kocevar November 22, 2021 Reading Time: approx. 3 min.

    Bernardo Berniluci’s 1970 political drama work The Conformist heavily emphasizes and portrays deep philosophical and societal themes within the work through the use of a mix of technical and artistic motifs. The confession scene (Timestamp: 27:20 to 34:12) within the middle of the first half of the movie encapsulates the use of these motifs perfectly. The scene follows protagonist Marcello’s promise to his soon-to-be wife Giulia to go to confession in preparation for their Catholic wedding. Marcello confesses to many sins during the confession, His most damning being murdering the offender of his childhood sexual assault, one which paints his character and explains his deep desire for conformity within the movie. The ideas of conformity, sin, and remorse are all used to help portray the character of Marcello more detailed, and also reflect inwards on Italian society itself.

    Berniluci’s use of the camera is a large element pertaining to the narrative and themes. When in confession, the priest talking to Marcello is shot only seeing his eyes through a small slit in the confessional, hiding in the shadows as Marcello confesses. The priest in part portrays Italian society and its perceived judgement towards Marcello, giving no empathy and urging him to continue. The camera shot at 31:50 exemplifies this view, with the priest forcing Marcello to continue his story while shaming him, with both members divided between the confessional wall. The priest embodies Marcellos thoughts and views on conformity. The priest amplifies the detriment of his sin speaking through the confessional, just as Marcello’s own thoughts in his head speak to him about societies views on his acts.

    Bernaluci’s use of lighting also plays into this motif, as the Marcello’s character is seen in broad light, but the priest, the embodiment of Marcello’s negative thoughts, is shrouded in the confessional in the shadows. The confessional is Marcellos mind, dark with trauma and childhood torment, dismissing his request to be let go and become a normal member of society. Even the cinematic language of the scene shows Marcello battling facial emotions of discomfort when asked to detail his story by the priest, to facial emotions of dismissal and triviality when arguing with the priest about regret and remorse for his sins.

    For the themes of the scene, it is clear that the namesake of the film, conformity, is a large underlying desire for Marcello. Marcello beleives his only possible escape from the past is gaining status in society, and conforming to the political machine of Italy. Marcello does not beg for forgiveness and feel remorseful, but rather is confused by the priest when asked if he feels contrite about what he has done, it is because alll Marcello desires is conformity. He smirks and says “One sin atones for another.” He believes his path to conformity is not in a confessional, but rather in the OVRA and secret police of Italy.

    This urge for normalcy and conformity is again perfectly shown by Bernalucci in the shot at 32:40, where again the shadow shrouded priest representing Marcello’s view of societies disdain for his acts and Marcello look upon his soon to be wife. Giulia is just another object in which he views as a way to achieve conformity and become a “normal” member of society, as he admits he is not even in love with her, and rather seeks normalcy. The cinematic language of the scene, the facial language of Marcello and the priest play heavily into the portrayal of the duality of Marcellos feelings. On one hand, Marcello seems to be happy, optimistic, and relieved. Marcello sees Giula as his way to obtain normalcy in Italian society. The inverse is portrayed in the priest. Again judgemental and unemotional, the priest highlights Marcellos doubt and underlying feelings that he will not be able to leave behind his trauma and obtain the normalcy he wants. The two portrayals of emotion within the image form the dialectic of Marcellos mind, the negative urge for conformity that is not believed to be obtainable, and the positive optimism for normalcy offered by the Fascist regime

    In conclusion, it is clear that Bernardo Berniluci’s film The Conformist heavily uses both technical and artistic motifs to address philosophical and societal themes in the scene the confession.

(Camera shot from 31:50, for reference.)

(Second camera shot from 32:40, for reference.)
entry.summary: An essay on the mise-en-scène of Bernardo Bernilucis political drama The Conformist.

entry.title: Human and Subhuman in M and Rome Open City

link.title: Human and Subhuman in M and Rome Open City

entry.content:
Human and Subhuman in M and Rome Open City

Jonathan Kocevar October 13, 2021 Reading Time: approx. 4 min.

    The 1930s to the 1940s were decades marked by the rise of authoritarianism, gruesome warfare, and horrific atrocities, and films of the era mirrored the same terrible acts into their narratives using overarching themes. One important theme often displayed is the idea of “human versus sub-human”. This theme is heavily used in both the German expressionist film M and the Italian neorealist film Rome Open City. Both films were produced during both the beginning and crumble of the regimes of the Third Reich and Fascist Italy, and both reflect the deeply ingrained social attitudes on race and the human/sub-human divisions held during the time, trying to tackle the racial dogma and encapsulate it inside the films.

    M and Rome Open City both heavily use the theme of the “sub-human” in “The Trial” and “The Salon” scenes respectively, with much of the set and narrative highlighting the theme to the audience. For example, in “The Trial” scene from M, the Mise-en-scène was set perfectly to emphasize the ostracization of the “sub-human” Hans Beckert from the group. Beckert is placed alone on his side of the set and is also placed physically lower than the judges and the jury, having to look up while on his knees and beg to the crowd. The film has no other sounds other than the argument within the kangaroo court, with the lone Beckert pleading from one side, and the unitary masses on the other chanting for his death, again pointing out the theme of the ostracized sub-human versus the group/humans. The scene “The Salon” in Rome Open City also portrays the theme of the “sub-human” from a different perspective, with the social rejection of the master-slave racial logic that plagued the decades before the making of the film within Fascist Italy and Nazi Germany. The scene is set within a salon filled with German high command, sharply dressed, surrounded by classical art and decor. The dialogue focuses on the argument about the German “master-race” between Major Bergmann and a cynical Major Hartmann. Major Hartmann rejects the racial arguments of Bergmann asserting that the subjugation of the people perceived to be “sub-humans” by Bergmann only leads to “unquenchable hatred” and that the Germans will die “without the slightest hope.” The diegetic piano music within the scene, combined with the renaissance decor of the set also reinforces the idea of high culture and civilization, many perceived characteristics of what the National Socialists and Fascists believed were parts of the “master-race” over “sub-humans”.

    Professor of Italian film studies and literature Peter Bondanella presents another take on the dialogue between Major Hartmann and Major Bergmann, stating that he believes that director Rosselini uses the dialogue to antagonize and critique the German attitude during the Nazi regime, Where Hartmann is portrayed as the first conscious German in the film who rejects the Nazi race science, dismissing Bergmann’s arguments. I personally agree with this opinion, as the context of the times in which the movie was made is during the end of the crumbling social dogma and false-race science that was engrained in both Italian and German society, and the antagonization of the occupying Germans by the Italians during the end of the war.

    While both M and Rome Open City seem very different in their portrayals of “superior/master humans over sub-humans”, both can also be interpreted with many connections and similarities. Both films highlight the rejection of master/slave, human/sub-human thought, and present the theme that people are equal and can’t be judged by isolated characteristics. In the trial scene in M, this argument takes the form of Beckert’s assigned defense counsel arguing that Schranker, the very judge accusing Beckert of being a crazed maniac who must be killed, was guilty of manslaughter himself, pointing out the contradictions of the human and superior judge and jury over sub-human criminal Beckert, asserting that they are not as different as they think. German poet and writer Horst Lange write about the convictions of Schranker during the trial scene in M, believing it not to be an argument from the standpoint of business and return to normalcy, but an emotionally charged argument, dividing himself from Beckert. Schranker is looking for a way to distance himself using emotional rhetoric, and creating a line between what acts are human and what acts are sub-human. This same theme of the portrayed subhuman as equal to the master-race is echoed within Rome Open City, with Major Hartmann stating that the enemies of Germany and their portrayed master-race were just as fierce and worthy as the Germans, and all their conquest in the name of superiority had gotten them was more enemies and casualties. Both films clearly portray the idea of equality between humans and races and reject the master-slave dichotomy of their time.

    In conclusion, Both the films M and Rome Open City tackle one of the most important social conflicts of their time and use the powerful theme of humans versus sub-humans to portray the collective attitudes of society during the rise and fall of Fascism and National Socialism on film.
entry.summary: An essay on the films M and Rome Open City, and the theme of violence and hatred.

entry.title: The Importance of the Revolutions of 1848 and the Paris Commune of 1871

link.title: The Importance of the Revolutions of 1848 and the Paris Commune of 1871

entry.content:
The Importance of the Revolutions of 1848 and the Paris Commune of 1871

Jonathan Kocevar June 8, 2020 Reading Time: approx. 8 min.

    Russian Revolutionary Vladimir I. Lenin once said: “There are decades where nothing happens; and there are weeks where decades happen.” His quote perfectly encapsulates the setting in which Europe was situated before both the Revolutions of 1848 and the Paris Commune of 1871. Unregulated capitalism encroached on major cities, tight societal strains from the censorship of press to limited suffrage had been ingrained in society since the days of the Napoleonic Wars, and authoritarian regimes blanketed the map of Europe. Festering and breeding within the common populace in Europe was reform and revolution, and the revolutions of 1848 and the Paris Commune would radically change the social, political, and economic institutions entrenched in Europe since the early 19th century. The Revolutions of 1848 and the Paris Commune of 1871 have been immense to the development of economic and social revolutionary processes in the century following. This is seen through radical divide from the ruling governments of the time toward liberal and egalitarian philosophy, the political impact on living revolutionaries writings and ideological doctrine of the time, and the influence on future revolutions and struggles in the early 20th century.

    Firstly, the focus of social and governmental reform and distance from the past regimes can be seen in both 1848 and The Paris Commune. The divide from the social and political status quo of the time and the embracement of egalitarian ideals and democracy can also be seen in the Paris Commune. On March 26, 1871, The Commune, Splitting away from the French Republic, only a week old and without governance, had its first elections.1 Instead of beginning to build a formidable military and advance to the French provisional republic’s capital in Versailles, The Communards decided to focus on the interior. They held elections, passed laws, and sent delegations to other cities to join the Commune. Proudonists, a group of Proto-Socialists and Anarchists, took the center of the early Commune, passing bills to outlaw the death penalty, to institute the abolition of the military draft, and stalling the backwater of rent built up during the siege of Paris to all Parisians.2 The Communard Central Council ordered all workshops to be collectivized, seized and maintained by the workers and also initiated the reorganization to begin. The Council called for the workshops to be turned into worker cooperatives, democratically electing workers to become foremen and managers. The stride taken by the central council was applauded by many Parisian workers, one speaker saying “The day of justice approaches with giant strides… the workshops in which you are packed will belong to you; the tools that are put into your hands will be yours; the gain resulting from your efforts, from your troubles, and from the loss of your health will be shared among you. Proletarians, you will be reborn.”3 With both the status of the economy and the structure of the government and law changed in the Commune, the status of women was next. During the Commune, the lives of women were improved tenfold to before the insurrection. Elisabeth Dmitrieff, Communard and founder of the Union des Femmes, was quoted as saying that the women of the Commune could achieve “the creation of a new social order” based upon “equality, solidarity and of freedom.”4 Women pressured the central council for attention to be put towards their rights and for gender equality, many of which would be helping defend against the Versailles government on the barricades along with their male counterparts weeks later.5 During the last week of the Commune, named Bloody Week by the Communards and Parisians, hope of the new social order for women was on the edge of being crushed. A group of female Communards, mostly radical feminists called the Petroleuses took to the streets and began to burn many historic buildings that tied France back to the days of Bourbon rule. They had the objective of cutting ties to the oppressive structures that had held progressives back for the last century of French history, and spiting the Versailles government.6 It is coherent that both the revolutionaries of 1848 and the Communards enacted much social and political change around Europe.

    Secondly, both revolutions would have an extreme impact on the ideologies and intellectual political radicals of the time, notably radical Socialist and Communist writers. Karl Marx, founder of communism and scientific socialism, was heavily influenced by his time both as a revolutionary during 1848, and as a witness to the 1871 Commune. In the aftermath of 1848 and the failed German Communist League and Belgian workers insurrection that Marx had supported fervently, Marx began to change his views and cement them in practical ideals learned during his work with the German Communist League. Marx saw from his time watching the revolutions of 1848 and believed that the only way to overthrow the capitalist threat was his idea of permanent revolution. In his book The Eighteenth Brumaire of Louis Bonaparte. Marx writes,

“As soon as one of the social strata above it gets into revolutionary ferment, the proletariat enters into an alliance with it and so shares all the defeats that the different parties suffer, one after another. But these subsequent blows become the weaker, the greater the surface of society over which they are distributed. The more important leaders of the proletariat in the Assembly and in the press successively fall victim to the courts, and ever more equivocal figures come to head it.“7

Marx believed that every time a revolution had begun, the working class and its representatives allied themselves with Liberal Democrats and the upper classes to overthrow the government, only to later take many of the blows in men and leadership and be betrayed by the upper class once a liberal democracy. Marx theorized this from his views of the February revolution of 1848 in France, which consolidated into a liberal democracy allowing Louis Napoleon III to take power, isolating many of the Socialist and Communist proletariat that helped to overthrow the July Monarchy. After the Bloody Week of 1871 in the Paris Commune, Marx and many other mainly Communist and radical Socialist writers began to change their views on state power and revolutionary doctrines. Marx specifically would assess some of his earlier Socialist and Communist works, most importantly the idea that “the working class cannot simply lay hold of the ready-made state machinery, and wield it for its own purposes.”8 Marx decided that for a revolution to succeed or strive, the workers must smash the state and replace it with their own rather than take the structure of the old state and fill it, directly modeled on the Commune. Russian anarchist Mikhail Bakunin, father of anarcho-collectivism and rival of Marx, saw the Commune as, “a bold, clearly formulated negation of the State.”9 Bakunin had reaffirmed his view of organization against the state, and tried to spark another commune uprising in Lyon in the same year. It is clear that both the revolutions of 1848 and the Paris Commune in 1871 were extremely impactful on the political philosophers of the time.

    Finally, the influence that the revolution of 1848 and the Paris commune had on future revolutions of the late 19th and early 20th century was enormous. The Revolutions of 1848, while being mostly repressed in Central Europe, had major effects on the state of France and the United States. In France, 1848 ushered an end to the July monarchy, and instituted a short-lived Second Republic, which would later consolidate in Louis Napoleon the third’s Second French Empire. The second empire would help unify Italy through various wars in the 1850s, liberating the people of Lombardy and Tuscany, aiding Italian nationalists to complete the Risorgimento, or plan to consolidate Italy. The Second empire would also take many initiatives to redesign Paris, turning it from a medieval city into a modern city, moving the impoverished Parisians to the outer arrondissements, or districts of Paris. This in turn caused strife and allowed revolutionary doctrines to be spread easier around the working men, being one of the main factors for the rise of the Paris Commune of 1871. The Revolutions of 1848 would be the precursor to the United States own revolution, the Civil war. After 1848, many of the revolutionaries became disillusioned with Europe after their goals were suppressed, and fled to the United States, where they would go on to preach for the freedom of Slavs in Austria, serfs in Russia, and the slaves in America.10 The Paris Commune influenced many socailist and Communist uprisings and revolutionary fervor. The most important event that the commune had influenced was the Russian revolutions of 1905 and 1917. The Revolution of 1905, caused by the loss of the Russians in the Russo-Japanese war saw many aspects taken from the Commune, notably the Chita Workers Republic in Siberia. The Revolution of 1917 saw the overthrow of the Tsars in Russia and leader of the Bolsheviks, Vladimir Lenin took many inspirations from the Commune. In his speech Lessons of the Commune, Lenin says,

“The Commune was a superb example of the great proletarian movement of the nineteenth century … The sacrifices of the Commune, heavy as they were, are made up for by its significance for the general struggle of the proletariat: it stirred the Socialist movement throughout Europe, it demonstrated the strength of civil war, it dispelled patriotic illusions, and destroyed the naïve belief in any efforts of the bourgeoisie for common national aims. The Commune taught the European proletariat to pose concretely the tasks of the Socialist revolution.“11

Commune iconography and ideas were highly idealized during the revolution and in the years after, with French Communists delivering Commune banners to the Russians and the Russians renaming the dreadnought Sevastopol to Parizhskaya Kommuna, or Paris Commune.12 In the early years of the Soviet union, the revolutionaries would try to model the country off of the Commune, but ultimately fail during Stalin’s reign.13 It is evident that the ideas and representation of the revolutions of 1848 and the Commune were both heavily influential on later revolutionary and reformist events.

    In conclusion, both the Revolutions of 1848 and the Paris Commune of 1871 were watershed moments for the social, economic, and political landscape of Europe. They would institute the divide from traditionalist governance towards democratic and egalitarian society, impact influential political ideologues and radical philosophers of the time, and would go on to determine and model later revolutionary struggles in the early 20th century.

Works Cited


  1. Donny Gluckstein, The Paris Commune: a Revolution in Democracy (Chicago: Haymarket Books, 2011). ↩

  2. Gluckstein, The Paris Commune: a Revolution in Democracy ↩

  3. John M. Merriman, Massacre: the Life and Death of the Paris Commune (New York, NY: Basic Books, 2014). ↩

  4. John Milner, Art, War and Revolution in France, 1870-1871: Myth, Reportage and Reality (New Haven: Yale University Press, 2000) ↩

  5. Merriman, Massacre: the Life and Death of the Paris Commune, 80 ↩

  6. Gay L. Gullickson, Unruly Women of Paris: Images of the Commune (Ithaca: Cornell University Press, 1996). ↩

  7. Karl Marx and Saul K Padover, “I,” in The Eighteenth Brumaire of Louis Bonaparte (Moscow, Central Federal District: Progress Publishers, 1937), pp. 9-9. ↩

  8. Karl Marx and Friedrich Engels, The Civil War in France (Mansfield Centre, CT: Martino Publishing, 2014). ↩

  9. Michail Aleksandrovic Bakunin, The Paris Commune and the Idea of the State (S.l.: B. Books, 1987). ↩

  10. Curti, Merle. “The Impact of the Revolutions of 1848 on American Thought.” Proceedings of the American Philosophical Society 93, no. 3 (1949) ↩

  11. Vladimir Ilʹich Lenin, in Lenin Collected Works, vol. 13 (Moscow: Progress Publishers, 1965), pp. 475-478. ↩

  12. Bergman, Jay. “The Paris Commune in Bolshevik Mythology.” The English Historical Review 129, no. 541 (2014): 1412-441. Accessed June 10, 2020 ↩

  13. Paul Saba, “The Paris Commune: First Proletarian Dictatorship,” The Paris Commune, accessed June 10, 2020 ↩
entry.summary: A political and historical analysis of the impact of both the liberal revolutions of 1848 and the Paris Commune.

entry.title: The Psychology of Radicalization
entry.content:
The Psychology of Radicalization

Jonathan Kocevar April 3, 2022 Reading Time: approx. 5 min.

    “The personal is political”, this infamous 60’s activist slogan could not be more correct when talking about radicalization. In the modern world every action is political; whether it be choosing what show to watch on TV or what to buy in a store. Making choices that incentivize one group over others, politics is the act of choosing how to enforce one’s interests. Politics dictate how our world acts and forms the basis for society. The contemporary political landscape has led to many marginalized groups feeling disenfranchised and gaining anti-establishment ideas and concepts. This is the basis of radicalization. The driving force for most political movements of the 19th and 20th centuries has been radicalization, be it the Bolsheviks or the Taliban. Understanding one’s motives and conditions of radicalization can aid professionals to halt violent radicalization or to understand the mechanisms which make one become radicalized and stabilize the conditions. With the recent rise of Islamic terrorist acts in the West and the re-emergence of white nationalism with far-right, domestic terrorist incidents, understanding and thwarting the threat of radicalization is extremely important.

    The field of political psychology and radicalism is a relatively new discipline that is beginning to grow in response to the rise of violent terror attacks and the attempt to halt them. One of the leading psychologists in the field of radicalization and terrorism is Robert Barret, a professional working with Nigerian terrorist cells. He follows active members and documents their actions and mindsets. Barret theorizes that there are six motivational forces that drive one to become politically radicalized; “the follower, the pragmatist, the criminal, the soldier, the basic needs, and the idealogue”.1 Barret uses these forces to underline basic methods and paths of extremism; examples include endorsement of ethnic supremacy as being “idealogue”, freedom to commit crime as the “criminal”, and supposed injustice within the system as the “soldier”. Barrett also researched the recruiting drives from radical groups and found that radicalized independants believed they were volunteering, with recruiters making “coercion feel like volunteerism”.1 John Horgan, a Pennsylvania State University psychologist, has stated that radicalization is not cause-effect or pathologic, rather that there is traits found that are advertently linked to radicalization; “the feeling of disenfranchisement, cynical and pessimistic of political involvement, identifying with victims of social injustice, believing that engaging violence against the state is not immoral, and joining a cell or movement heightened sense of identity”.2 The important point that Horgan states is that the paths are not cause and effect, individuals can associate with traits of radicalization, but not be radical, which makes the topic of extremism in the psychological community less about why does someone become radicalized and rather how does society stop or minimize the risk factors that lead to paths of extremism and radicalization.

    The identifiers and signals hypothesised by psychologists like John Horgan and Robert Barret allow for specialists to try to thwart violent attacks and halt radicalization of groups. Understanding the psychology of what causes one to join a radical group or terrorist organization and stopping the paths that allow one to become radicalized is more effective than suppression and direct contact with the group. In the late eighties, a Peruvian Maoist insurgency group named “Shining Path” occupied many districts of Peru and was a major political force inside the country, with thousands of members and high ranking officials working in the school districts interacting with the youth.3 In the late 1990’s, the Peruvian government used psychology to solve the problem by cutting off the recruitment paths that the radicalized used to indoctrinate and influence others, raiding the school boards, infiltrating neighborhoods, and seeking and isolating the Maoists. Once disconnected from all sources of recruitment, and with high officials in prison, the Shining Path declined immensely.4 Another government combating radicalization is in Aarhus, Denmark. The “Aarhus Model” seeks to stop young people from joining Islamic extremist groups such as ISIS or Al-Qaeda by giving counselling and monitoring services for the radicalized.5 By giving the potentially radicalized support and understanding their mindset, the individuals are able to integrate into society better than enforcing strict crackdown policies. The radicalized gain support and form a bond of trust between authority and the individual, instead of the individual antagonizing authority.

    The future of the study of political psychology and radicalization is the only way of permanently lowering the population of radicalized people. Suppression of thought, denial of solving the roots of radicalization, oppression and physical force only delays insurgent efforts, but ensures that the force will become more omnipresent.6 The study of political psychology intends to do the opposite, realizing the only way to effectively stop insurgency is by cutting down the communities radicals influence and recruit from. In Louise Richardson’s book, “What Terrorists Want: Understanding the Enemy, Containing the Threat”, Richardson argues that to undermine the threat of radicalization and terrorism, the governing body must “Remove the Terrorists from their Communities”. Richardson states that with the knowledge of what causes a person to become radicalized, or rather the modifiers which allow conversion to become easier, that cause should be removed from the community to lower the force multiplier of one becoming radicalized, subsequently lowering the percentage of an individual becoming radicalized from their community. Richardson’s’s concept of isolation of terrorists from communities could be by the presence of the United States Military in Afghanistan and Iraq. Since the United States Military entrance into the Middle East, US officials have failed to stop the terrorist and radical cells from indoctrinating and recruiting communities with “us” vs “them” sentiments. The study of radicalization is the embodiment of the behaviour of humans. As aforementioned, politics is the enforcement of one’s ideals; the struggle of enforcing ideals that counter the existing ideas implemented in society and engrained in institutions and is the driving force of political strife and radicalization. In a society where every action is political, select members of that society will feel marginalized by the society’s actions or ideas. Radicalization shows how the interaction of those marginalized individuals choose to combat the established order, groups form for what each member of that society deems is the best for their situation, whether that be a political party or a far-right or left vanguard insurgency to see their ideals enforced.

    In conclusion, the developments of political psychology and radicalization are crucial to stopping violent attacks globally and halting the formation of insurgent groups that are a threat to safety. It is clear that the theories psychologists have implemented in the past were successful, showing that learning the paths and methods of extremism can allow permanent solutions to the problems of radicalization and are the clearest way of both helping the marginalized and curbing the threat of extremism in the future.

Works Cited


  1. Barrett, R. S. (2011). Interviews with Killers: Six Types of Combatants and Their Motivations for Joining Deadly Groups. Studies in Conflict & Terrorism, 34(10), 749–764. doi: 10.1080/1057610x.2011.604830 ↩ ↩2

  2. Horgan, J. (2009). Walking away from terrorism: accounts of disengagement from radical and extremist movements. London: Routledge. ↩

  3. The Shining Path. (n.d.) ↩

  4. Coha. (2008, June 5). The Rise and Fall of Shining Path. ↩

  5. How to prevent violent extremism and radicalisation? (n.d.) ↩

  6. Anderson, A. J., Anderson, V., Balcazar, F. E., Berardi, L., Bernai, I., Boddapati, S., … Zinn, M. (2019, June 21). Oppression and Power. ↩
entry.summary: An essay on how terrorism and radicalism find thier roots in society, from a psychological standpoint.

entry.title: On the Issue of Supply-Side Economics

link.title: On the Issue of Supply-Side Economics

entry.content:
On the Issue of Supply-Side Economics

Jonathan Kocevar January 21, 2020 Reading Time: approx. 11 min.

Introduction

    “The rich get richer and the poor get poorer”, an infamous quote by Percey Shelly paints an exemplary picture of one of the major discourses in the modern macroeconomic debate, the support of Supply-side Economics. The theory was sparked from the question of what the most effective theory and policy was to implement in a country to stimulate growth. The theory essentially argues that market regulation and taxation should be rescinded to allow for the private sector to develop the market instead of the government. The theory of Supply-side Economics is an ineffective way of stimulating economic growth and historically has caused many problems where it has been tried. Through the analysis of its origin and history, negative application in modern economics and criticizing the proponents of Supply-side Economics, it is clear that Supply-side Economics is not the fit macroeconomic policy for the modern political landscape.

The History and Origin of the Supply-Side Theory

    The Macroeconomic Theory of Economics has its roots during the stagflation and recession of the mid-1970s, carrying all the way into the early 1980s.1 The economic woes were dealt from the slowly receding economic development and growth of the post-World War II eras. This issue combined with the oil crises that hit America in the 1970s, left the American Government to turn to radically new macroeconomic ideas and different approaches to the relationship between the government and domestic businesses. In the early 1980s, economist and member of President Reagan’s Economic Policy Advisory Board, Arthur Laffer began to theorize the core arguments for a new economic theory which would later be called Supply-side Economics. The main theory being that overall economic growth can most effectively be encouraged through the act of lowering commercial taxes and decreasing regulation within the industrial and commercial sectors.

    The core idea was that this would begin to solve the economic problems that had been facing the United States during the recession and hit President Ford’s and President Reagan’s economic goals, such as price stability, full employment and the expansion of economic freedom. This was a very radical difference compared to the macroeconomic systems of the 1960s and earlier, where most governments took a Keynesian, Demand-Side policy, focusing on the stimulation of the economy through monetary stimulus and heavy government involvement in the markets. (examples including the New Deal of the 1930s, and the Postwar Marshall Plan). Today, the argument of Supply-side vs Demand-Side economics is still a point of major contention. Crises such as the 2007-2008 recession and the 2020 Covid-19 pandemic have been important forums and topics of debate for economists on the most adaptable and stable macroeconomic policy and has highlighted many problems in the Supply-side Policy, such as needed stimulus for businesses to stay afloat and tax funding to revitalizing the public and private sector.

Arguments against Supply-Side Economics

    First, the social equality and general economical aspects of supply-side economics and Reaganomics are flawed and deeply rooted in incorrect premises. Reaganomics, and the idea of trickle-down economics, is one core idea of the Supply-Side theory. Trickle-down economics supposes that the government should loosen tax laws on businesses and the wealthy within the country, as a way to encourage stimulus in the form of reinvestment and through the funding of works by investors to in turn help the public and meeting economic goals proposed by the government through private investors instead of direct government spending. The idea is still widely seen as a good theory to solving economic problems within conservative circles and had been used extensively during Trump’s term as the President of the United States.2 The idea of trickle-down economics encouraging development has been widely researched throughout the 1980s, and 1990s, with some researchers studying its effects up to the present day.34 Economist David Hope of the London School of Economics and Julian Limberg of King’s College, London had studied the policy of trickle-down economics from the 1970s to 2015, and wrote in their paper:

“We find that major reforms reducing taxes on the rich lead to higher income inequality as measured by the top 1% share of pre-tax national income. The effect remains stable in the medium term. In contrast, such reforms do not have any significant effect on economic growth and unemployment.”5

The paper states that no correlation exists between private investment in the public sector and aid to the poor. It essentially states that the tax money that the government released back to the wealthy 1%, had not gone to investment but rather to other means for the rich to accumulate even more of the wealth in the economy. A study from the Tax Justice Network (TJN) shows that the tax cuts to the rich did not go to rebuilding and reinvesting into the economy, but rather to be accumulated in overseas tax havens and tax shelters, serving no use to the domestic economy. The leader of the research at the TJN, John Christensen said about the issue:

“Inequality is much, much worse than official statistics show, but politicians are still relying on trickle-down to transfer wealth to poorer people … This new data shows the exact opposite has happened: for three decades extraordinary wealth has been cascading into the offshore accounts of a tiny number of super-rich.”

The effects of this wealth hoarding have been extremely harmful and detrimental towards the middle and lower working class of not only America but every developed nation’s economy that had been affected by the theory of trickle-down economics. Research conducted by the Economic Policy Institute summated in a graph shows the overwhelming wealth disparity.

(For Image Credit, See6)

The research shows that the top 1% (the red), and the Bottom 90% (the light blue) have progressed from around the same amount of wealth shared (~35%) before Reagan’s term, where progressive tax rates implemented by previous administrations were still present (near a 70% marginal rate). When Reagan began his term in 1981, his tax breaks implemented only a year into his first term, lowering from the 70% top bracket marginal tax rate to 50% marginal tax rate.7 The Pre-Reagan rates have never gone back to since, which has allowed the rich to accumulate and snowball their wealth. By the end of Reagan’s Presidency, the national tax rate on top-bracket earners would be 28%, a whopping 60% decrease.7 Not only have the ideals of Supply-side Economics failed to meet their proposed economic goals, such as price stability from the rising inflation of the 70s, full employment, and the expansion of economic freedom to corporations but have also deepened other economic problems such as the negative Equitable Distribution of Income. The arguments for the support of Supply-side Economics and support of Trickle-down Economics is factually unfounded.

    Second, another problem with the theory of Supply-side Economics is that one of its sole purposes, encouraging economic goals through the use of the private sector, does not happen. Productivity being the first major goal, the idea that lowering commercial and top bracket tax rates will allow business owners and investors to reinvest into better technology to improve productivity and ultimately push the production possibility curve to the right. Investment growth being the second goal, the idea that the tax rescinded dollars would go towards reinvestment in new firms or into the expansion of firms. Both these goals were stunted when the Supply-side policy was introduced, leading to inefficiency and lagging of the domestic economy.

(For Image Credit, See8)

Both productivity and reinvestment being major important aspects to the theory, it is unexpected to see the following results. Both worker productivity and investment growth had been less in supply side economies, versus Demand-side economies, which had a better effect in both categories. Investment growth had more than a 9% increase in the demand-side model, even when accounting for the 1990s recession.8 Two of Supply-sides major premises had been impeded on and limited by the economic policies of the supply-side model.

    Third, the issue of Supply side Economics did not only affect the efficiency of industry and the commercial sector, but also affected the government’s ability to spend and was attributed largely to the beginning of the increase of debt that would soon later skyrocket. The annotated graph below shows this exact increase in debt.

(For Image Credit, See9)

It can be argued that public debt rise itself is not a bad thing necessarily, as long as the government has enough to pay it off as their capital markets expand with them, but under Reagan and his economic policy the debt held as a percentage of the GDP increased to over 30%. That is not only the public debt, but it is compared to the GDP and the capital markets available to the United States Government. The result of this was an uninterrupted ride increasing the public debt that the US continues to see today (other than a short term under Clinton). The tax cuts and deregulation that Reagan had enforced in 1981-82 did in reality not only do relatively nothing to kickstart the economy, but actually damaged the economy by adding large deficits to the federal budget to spend more on defense by spending money they no longer had10, but also removed many small business funding programs and grants that had allowed the businesses to thrive.11 It is perspicuous that Reagan’s Supply-side model had not only hurt the economy, but also the federal budget and debt as well.

The Opposition, The Arguments for Supply-Side Economics

    One of the major arguments for Supply-side Economics is its historical use during Reagan’s presidency and its sentiment among supporters as a major factor in fixing the recessions of the 1970s and helping supercharge the economy in the following years. Many supporters of Reaganomics policies will turn toward the 1980s and look at the correlation between Reagan’s tax cuts for the top bracket and the Real GDP of the United States to explain the economic revitalization of the US during the 80s12 such as the ones below.

(For Image Credit, See13)

(For Image Credit, See14)

While it is true that an economic rebound did take place during Reagan’s terms, the reasons for the rebound are deeper than his Supply-side policies. As learned in class, interest rates are one of the most important tools and stimulators of the economy that the government has available. Many attribute the growth of the real GDP to Reagan’s tax cuts and in turn the economic stimulation of the economy by the wealthy business owners and investors when in reality, this had little effect. The larger effect was the Federal Reserve’s responses to interest rates during the early 1980s. In 1981, the Federal Reserve bumped interest rates up to almost 20%, to facilitate low spending and risk in the economy, halting it to fix the inflation of the 1970s, as money will not circulate if there is too much risk to invest in the market. This led to a larger recession, which the rebound that would hit the next year is the recession Reagan is usually accredited to by supporters of Supply-side theory. The more probable cause is that when the Federal Reserve would lower the rates back to 13% in 1982, confidence was gained again by investors and banks to begin trading and lending money again. The annotated graph below again helps explain how the Real GDP growth correlates with the Federal Reserve raising of the interest rates.

(For Image Credit, See15)

The recession of 1981 can be attributed to the Federal Reserves raising interest rates, and many economic historians agree on the fact that the market upturn was majorly in response to the lowering of the interest rates again causing the markets to rescind from isolation and begin trading and dealmaking again. This Real GDP rise in 1982 also compounded with the decrease of inflation caused by the Federal Reserve raising interest rates.16 It is clear that the arguments supporting the use of Supply-side Economics through argumentation of the history of the theory and its implementation in Reagan’s economic policy and US markets upturn are unreasonable and unfactual.

Relation to the Local Economy

    The issue of Supply-side economics is deeply rooted within Ontario, with one of the most adamant of supporters and theorizers for Supply-side economics being Kingston-born Canadian Economist Robert Mundell. Many of the policies taken into account during Reagan’s and some of Kennedy’s administrations were directly modeled on what Mundell believed should have happened to the East and West of Canada, especially in the Southern Ontario car sector and the Northern Ontario lumber sector. These industries crossed borders a numerous amount of times throughout Canada and America and Mundell saw the tax rates and government regulations as a damage to the fluidity of the trades between the two states.17 The proposals of using supply-side policy when it comes to the border is a double edge sword. While it does reduce costs in taxation and reduces prerequisites to ship cross country, it also brings along easy crime and disease, and also forces many domestically made products out of the country and forces more imports in turn, allowing for American domination of the Canadian economy. Toronto has also had one of the largest problems with the ideals of Supply-side policies within the city. The housing market of the city has been on the rise for years and shows no sign of slowing down. The chart below shows that since 2012, real estate value in Toronto has nearly doubled. The reason for this is the lack of government regulation and intervention in the process.

(For Image Credit, See18)

The regional municipality of Toronto had pushed a motion to require at least three years’ supply of serviced or ready land for a range of different residential buildings.19 Today, the metropolitan government follows these rules loosely if not at all and it has led the Toronto housing market to spiral.20 The creation of new single-detached family homes has gone from 30,000 units during 2001, to 15.000 units in 2014, leading to a large demand in one-family residential plots.20 This problem of non-government intervention is not only seen in the single-family residential sector but also in the condominium and apartment markets. This loose government grasp on plot allocation combined with the low-interest rates have left for large demand on leasing and buying a property with enticing market possibilities within Toronto, but the libertarian Supply-side attitude by the policymakers have left Toronto’s economic possibilities short.21 It is clear that even on the local scale, Supply-side Economics sells short the opportunity and market prospects of the Ontario area.

Conclusion

    To summarize, through analysis and argumentation of the origins and background of Supply-side economics, criticism of the proponents of Supply-side theory and examination of effects on the local economy, it is implicit that Supply-side Economics is an ineffective and harmful macroeconomic strategy when applied to the modern real world. Social satirist and journalist H.L. Mencken once wrote, “For every complex problem there is an answer that is clear, simple, and wrong.” It perfectly encapsulates the macroeconomic theory of Supply-side policy and its harmful effects on the complex and captivating study that is contemporary economics and that it is not fit for the modern political and economic landscape of the 21st century.

Works Cited


  1. James Chen, “Supply-Side Theory Definition,” Investopedia (Investopedia, January 15, 2021) ↩

  2. Noah Smith, “Trump Puts Supply-Side Economics to Its Final Test,” Bloomberg.com (Bloomberg, October 10, 2018) ↩

  3. Craig Stirling, “Fifty Years of Tax Cuts for Rich Didn’t Trickle Down, Study Says,” December 15, 2020 ↩

  4. Nicole Lewis, “Analysis Did Ronald Reagan’s 1981 Tax Cut Supercharge the Economy?,” The Washington Post (WP Company, August 15, 2018) ↩

  5. David Hope and Julian Limberg, “The Economic Consequences of Major Tax Cuts for the Rich,” The London School of Economics and Political Science, December 2020 ↩

  6. Economic Snapshot • By Josh Bivens • April 23, “The Top 1 Percent’s Share of Income from Wealth Has Been Rising for Decades,” Economic Policy Institute, April 23, 2014 ↩

  7. James Fallows, “When the Top U.S. Tax Rate Was 70 Percent-or Higher,” The Atlantic (Atlantic Media Company, January 26, 2019) ↩ ↩2

  8. Michael Ettlinger and Michael Linden, “The Failure of Supply-Side Economics,” Center for American Progress, October 25, 2012 ↩ ↩2

  9. “How Much Is the National Debt? What Are the Different Measures Used?,” Peter G. Peterson Foundation, accessed January 18, 2021 ↩

  10. “Commanding Heights : Reaganomics on PBS,” PBS (Public Broadcasting Service, 1998) ↩

  11. Ronald Brownstein, “Reagan’s Squeeze on Small Business,” The Christian Science Monitor (The Christian Science Monitor, December 29, 1981) ↩

  12. Dan Mitchell, “Was Reaganomics a Success?,” International Liberty, May 12, 2016 ↩

  13. James Fallows, “When the Top U.S. Tax Rate Was 70 Percent-or Higher,” The Atlantic (Atlantic Media Company, January 26, 2019) ↩

  14. Kimberly Amadeo, “An Annual Review of the U.S. Economy Since 1929,” The Balance, March 13, 2020 ↩

  15. “Effective Federal Funds Rate,” FRED Economic Data, January 4, 2021 ↩

  16. Kimberly Amadeo, “US Inflation Rate by Year from 1929 to 2023,” The Balance, October 26, 2020 ↩

  17. Brian Domitrovic, “Trade With Canada Is The Origin Of Supply-Side Economics,” Forbes (Forbes Magazine, October 1, 2018) ↩

  18. Randi Emmott, “Toronto Real-Estate Market,” Toronto Real Estate Market Statistics by Randi Emmott, accessed January 18, 2021 ↩

  19. David Amborski and Frank Clayton, “The Impact of Supply-Side Economics on Toronto’s Rising House Prices,” The Globe and Mail, October 31, 2015 ↩

  20. Bloomberg News, “Tight Toronto Housing Market Pushes Prices to Record Highs with Biggest Gains in Three Years,” financialpost (Financial Post, January 7, 2020) ↩ ↩2

  21. Colin McClelland, “Canadian Mortgage Rates Are the Lowest in History. Can They Go Any Lower?,” financialpost (Financial Post, September 28, 2020) ↩
entry.summary: An essay on the rejection of the macroeconomic theory of a supply-side market and Reaganomics.

entry.title: Materialism and Naturalism: The Harmony of Marx and Aristotle

link.title: Materialism and Naturalism: The Harmony of Marx and Aristotle

entry.content:
Materialism and Naturalism: The Harmony of Marx and Aristotle

Jonathan Kocevar January 6, 2020 Reading Time: approx. 10 min.

    Karl Marx and Aristotle were both realists and materialists, science being held to the highest degree when forming their theories and philosophies. Both were born into chaotic and shifting societies during times of crisis and found philosophy as a way to explain and solve practical problems that faced them every day. which would shape their ideas, whether it is the constantly battling city-states or the rise of industrial capitalism in Western Europe. Through the analysis of both philosopher’s thoughts on ethics and alienation, metaphysics and view on religion, it is clear that the ideas of Karl Marx and Aristotle hold many similarities and share many ideas, even with being separated by over 2000 years.

    The historical and local backgrounds of both of the philosophers share extremely similar environments that had affected the light in which they thought and formulated their theories. Karl Marx was born in the Prussian city of Trier during 1818, a rapid time of change in Europe after the fall of Napoleon a short three years earlier. Napoleon’s campaigns had left Europe in scrambles rebuilding itself and the ideological embers created by the French Revolution waiting to be sparked once more. Marx’s family was very deeply rooted in those embers— his father being a heavy supporter of the idealism of men like Kant and Voltaire and the ideas of the enlightenment, which would carry into Marx’s formative years and change many of his fundamental views. Marx’s early life aligned with the beginning of the major powers of Europe developing the means of labor, such as more advanced machinery and tools, a time period known as the Industrial Revolution, and the major focus of his works and ideology. Marx saw firsthand the shift from artisan crafting of singular items to mass, factory-scale commodity production owned by the rich or bourgeois, which he saw as a major problem for the new poor industrial working class, called the proletariat. Marx had formulated that the proletariat was slowly becoming isolated from its labor and work and being controlled by any means, whether it be organized religion, the law, or bourgeois government propaganda. Nearly 1500 years before Marx, the ancient Greek philosopher Aristotle was born in 384 BC in the northern city-state of Stagira in Greece. Much like the political culture and geopolitics of Marx’s formative years, Greece had been torn and left destroyed in the Peloponnesian war only 20 years before his birth, which would go on to be conquered by the Macedonians and forced to accept the non-homogenous rule of foreigners and their conflicting traditions and concepts. Not only did the occupation bring the cultures and radical ideas of the northern Macedonians to Greece, causing conflict in the marketplace of ideas, but also created a time of peace which led to great philosophical and political growth, allowing the young Aristotle to enroll at Plato’s Academy and join the philosophical practice within Greece until 347 BC, the year of Plato’s death. In 335, Aristotle would create his own academy labeled the Lyceum, where he would focus on a wide range of subjects, especially subjects such as the history of philosophy, metaphysics, psychology, theology, and political philosophy, the same subjects that Marx would grasp and try to solve during his time.

    Both Marx and Aristotle had extremely similar views on the matter of both organized religion and the application of faith towards a society. Aristotle was a very staunch critic of organized religion throughout his life, being a naturalist first and foremost, the study and use of god were practical rather than faithful in his worldview. Aristotle also critiqued the way religion was used practically by the leading figures of his time, such as politicians and military leaders, as a way to coerce the population into following their orders. In his book Politics, Aristotle writes:

“A tyrant must put on the appearance of uncommon devotion to religion. Subjects are less apprehensive of illegal treatment from a ruler whom they consider god-fearing and pious. On the other hand, they do less easily move against him, believing that he has the gods on his side.”1

    Aristotle is stating that who he believed to be the strong tyrant rulers of his time used religion as a tool against political opponents and as a way to pacify the average citizen. Even if the tyrant was not believing or faithful, Aristotle saw organized religion as a major tool authority could use to push individuals to act a certain way. Marx would have agreed entirely with Aristotle’s analysis of Organized Religion, even further expanding on it in his own writings. In his work A Contribution to the Critique of Hegel’s Philosophy of the Right, Marx infamously proclaims “Religion is the Opiate of the People”. While often misunderstood and misquoted, Marx was directly building upon Aristotle’s critique of organized religion as nothing more than a controller of the masses. Rosie Blau of the Economist perfectly encapsulates Marx’s view on religion and his well-known quote:

“Marx was not exactly against religion. For him, faith was something that “the people” conjured for themselves, a source of phony happiness to which they turned to help numb the pain of reality. It was “the sigh of the oppressed creature”. Organized religion with its churches, doctrines and priests followed on from that, a useful tool by which the ruling classes kept the masses supine.”2

Both Marx and Aristotle held very cynical and critical views upon organized religion, but both also had understood the power of local, horizontally structured, non-hierarchical religion. In Marx’s work The Economic and Philosophic Manuscripts of 1844, he writes, “To develop in greater spiritual freedom, a people must break their bondage to their bodily needs—they must cease to be the slaves of the body. They must, above all, have time at their disposal for spiritual creative activity and spiritual enjoyment.”3 Marx saw communal religion and spirituality as an aid in an individual’s freedom and dissociation of past bourgeois institutions and saw it as an improvement tenfold compared to the organized religion and church apparatuses of his day. Aristotle, while being the critic of organized religion as nothing more than a powerbase for tyrants, like Marx held that religion and spirituality itself was not a bad thing. In the centuries following his death and the rise of Christianity, Aristotle’s metaphysics and ethics would go on the form the framework for the basis of Christian theology and ontology.4 In Aristotle’s ethics book Nicomachean Ethics, the words ‘god’ and ‘divine’ occur roughly twice as often as the words ‘happiness’ and ‘happy’, it directly equivocates that a form of god is at the very center of Aristotle’s ethical philosophy and that happiness is gained from it, again implying the innate goodness of non-organized religion.5 Both Karl Marx and Aristotle’s views and philosophies on religion were highly critical of the institution of religion, and both agreed on the fact of communal religion and spirituality being an emancipator of the individual overall.

    Both philosophers shared many of the same philosophical concepts in their metaphysical theories, Marx’s being his philosophy of science, and Aristotle’s being his Natural Realism. In direct contrast to his mentor and teacher Plato, Aristotle turned from the idealist worldview of the Academy and began creating his own metaphysical framework of the world, which would take the form of Natural Realism. To Aristotle, the immaterial was not of direct use when understanding the world around him. He was against the use of the theory of forms as an explanation to the world and began to look directly in the physical world around him. Aristotle would use this realist worldview to explain many phenomena through science, by using the scientific formula.6 In direct contrast to his recent philosopher predecessors such as Kant, Marx began his metaphysical theory based on the reading of Hegel and interpolation of Hegel’s theory of dialectics. Marx would go on to make his theory of science within Marxism and the dialectic to form the concrete groundwork for his materialist claims.
